title: What happens when agents get their own computers
summary: Jonas Nelle demonstrates Cursor 3's agent capabilities: autonomous code testing with video proof-of-work and cloud agents that manage pull requests to merge-ready state. The system removes human bottlenecks in build-and-ship cycles by replacing manual steps with checkpoints and cloud environments developers can control directly.
writeup: Jonas from Cursor demos two concrete workflow features that convert blocking human checkpoints in the software development lifecycle into asynchronous agent handoffs. The first removes the build-to-test bottleneck by having a cloud agent generate a video proof of its own test run, so a developer reviews a completed, evidenced PR rather than raw uncommitted changes. The second removes the PR-to-merge bottleneck via a "babysit PR in cloud" button that spins up a new cloud agent, seeded with the local agent's context, to fix CI failures, address merge conflicts, and resolve review comments autonomously. The organizing principle is that agents should own entire SDLC segments, not just isolated coding steps, so human judgment enters at intentional, high-value moments rather than as a default blocker.

similar.title: The next era of AI coding

Claude Managed Agents Will Make Millionaires (do this now)

key_points: Cursor cloud agents now produce video artifacts (and optionally screenshots or logs) as proof of test execution, letting a reviewer watch the feature work before reading a single line of diff.
The developer can take live control of the cloud dev server the agent ran, inspecting edge cases and interacting with the running app directly from Cursor web without any local environment.

The 'babysit PR in cloud' button clones a locally running agent into a cloud VM with its full context and instructs it to fix CI, address merge conflicts, and resolve review comments, freeing the developer for higher-value work during the typical 10-minute CI wait.
Cloud agents are not tied to the developer's local machine: the laptop could be discarded and the agent continues, and multiple such agents can run in parallel on separate PRs.

Cursor frames these as two illustrative instances of a broader category: many SDLC bottlenecks can be handed off to agent teams, and the company is shipping both built-in product features and lower-level primitives for organizations to build their own agent systems.
